brief_justification: Paper A is an algebraic-statistics identifiability result: it recovers a discrete phylogenetic-network topology from a leaf-pattern probability distribution generated by a Markov substitution model, and shows networks leave a detectable signature distinguishing them from trees. Paper B is an algebraic/symplectic-geometry study that gives local descriptions of Coulomb branches via blowups of toric compactifications and classifies their symplectic leaves and transverse slices. There is no shared transferable machinery — statistical identifiability of a distribution's parameters versus geometric stratification of a symplectic variety — and the apparent overlap ('blobs' vs 'leaves', 'classification') is only vocabulary, so no role-for-role mapping holds.
